main_sections.title: Feathered Friend
main_sections.prose_summary:
Feathered Friend is a science fiction short story by Arthur C. Clarke, narrated in the first person by an unnamed officer aboard a space station. The story opens by establishing that no regulation forbade pets on the station, though no one had thought to make one either. Sven Olsen, one of the station's best construction workers — skilled at manoeuvring steel girders in free fall — had smuggled aboard a small yellow canary named Claribel, hiding her during the ferry journey from Earth. His stated reason was scientific curiosity: he wanted to observe how a bird would behave in zero gravity. Claribel quickly adapted, learning to float and loop through the station with effortless grace. She became a communal favourite, concealed from visiting VIPs in the station's many hiding places, though her occasional noisy peeps from ventilating shafts caused some anxious moments.

The story shifts to a morning when Claribel is found unconscious — two clenched claws pointing pathetically upwards. The ship's cook-doctor-dietitian, Jock Duncan, cannot confirm a heartbeat. The crew revives her with oxygen from an emergency cylinder, but she collapses again immediately after perching on Sven's finger and giving her signature trill. The narrator, whose mind feels sluggish and headachy, suddenly recalls that miners historically carried canaries into coal mines to detect dangerous gases. He alerts the duty engineer Jim, who dismisses the warning confidently — until his assistant points out that the second alarm circuit was never connected. Jim leaves without a word. The air purifier had frozen during a rare eclipse of Earth's shadow, and the single connected alarm had silently failed. Half a million dollars of engineering had been defeated; without Claribel, the crew would have died. The narrator ends with a wry recommendation: any space station visitor who hears an inexplicable snatch of birdsong should feel doubly reassured rather than alarmed — it means the station has a living early-warning system at practically no extra cost. The story's central theme is the value of unexpected, low-tech solutions in high-tech environments, and the quiet heroism of a creature whose sensitivity to danger saved an entire crew.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Work in pairs and discuss the questions given below. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: Who can a 'feathered' friend be?
Which 'feathered' friend do you like? Why?
If you have a choice between a 'feathered' and a 'furry' friend, who would you choose? Why?
Do you think a 'feathered' or a 'furry' friend can accompany astronauts to space? If yes, why? If no, why not?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the words given below by inserting vowels. Refer to their meanings given alongside. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: official rule that controls how something is done: R _ G _ L _ T _ _ N
to not allow something: F _ R B _ D
join or blend to form a single entity: F _ S _
suddenly: _ B R _ P T L Y
admit one's mistake: C _ N F _ S S
a person who advises what to eat to keep healthy: D_ _ T _ T _ _ N

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions briefly.

question_bank.stem: Why did the narrator think Sven had sneaked a bird aboard? What was Sven's scientific reason?
How did Claribel adjust to the new surroundings?
What made the narrator mistake the musical whistle for a sound from the intercom?
Do you think the presence of the canary would lead the spacers into trouble? If yes, why? If no, why not?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify whether the following statements from parts I and II are true or false. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: The alarm at the space station failed to work properly because they were not connected.
Claribel was brought to the space station for an official experiment on animal behaviour in space.
The canary's suffering helped the crew discover a problem with the air purifier.
The air purifier froze because there was a rare eclipse.
Claribel could fly gracefully in the space station, performing loops in the air.
The space station crew was uninterested in Claribel and hardly noticed her presence after she was discovered.
Claribel had fainted and had to be revived with oxygen supply.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: Sven was one of our best construction men, and excelled at the tricky and specialised work of collecting assorted girders as they floated around in free fall, making them do the slow-motion, three-dimensional ballet that would get them into their right positions, and fusing the pieces together when they were precisely dovetailed into the intended pattern: it was a skilled and difficult job, for a space suit is not the most convenient of garbs in which to work.

question_bank.stem:
What can be inferred about Sven's abilities from his job description?
A. He is good at following instructions but lacks creativity.
B. He possesses a strong understanding of working in space.
C. He struggles with the complexities of construction in space.
D. He prefers working on simpler tasks rather than complex jobs.
How can we conclude that Sven was working in zero gravity?

Select the option that is correct for both Assertion (A) and Reason (R).
(A): Sven's job required him to make girders perform a 'three-dimensional ballet' in space.
(R): Working in zero gravity allows objects to float, making precise positioning more challenging.
A. Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
B. Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).
C. (A) is true, but (R) is false.
D. (A) is false, but (R) is true.
Replace the underlined word with a word similar in meaning from the extract. During the festival, people wore colourful outfits that represented their culture. [underlined: outfits]

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: "Jim! There's something wrong with the air! That's why Claribel's passed out. I've just remembered that miners used to carry canaries down to warn them of gas." "Nonsense!" said Jim. "The alarms would have gone off. We've got duplicate circuits, operating independently." "Er—the second alarm circuit isn't connected up yet," his assistant reminded him. That shook Jim; he left without a word, while we stood arguing and passing the oxygen bottle around like a pipe of peace.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blank by selecting the correct option from those given in the brackets. The narrator references canaries and miners in order to __________. (highlight the historical use of canaries as warning systems / explain the reason that caused Claribel to faint suddenly)
Complete the following sentence with a suitable reason. Jim initially dismisses the warning about the air because __________.

What does the phrase 'shook Jim' mean in the context of the information in the extract? Jim was _________.
A. physically pushed by someone
B. startled by unexpected information
C. cold and needed to warm up
D. trying to shake off sleepiness
What does the crew's action of passing the oxygen bottle around 'like a pipe of peace' suggest about their response to the situation?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What was the purpose of the alarm? What had caused its failure?
How does the crew's reaction to Claribel change over the course of the story?
What does the last part of the story tell us about the significance of unusual ways of problem solving?
Justify the appropriateness of the title of the story.
'Without Claribel, we should soon have been slightly dead.' What does this line suggest about uncertainty of life in space?
Explain how has the writer presented science fiction with a touch of humour.
How would Sven reflect on his experience of almost losing Claribel and for saving the lives of the entire crew?

listening.transcript_text:
Monika: Hi, Toby! Do you miss your planet?
Toby: Very much, Monika! I miss my people and home.
Monika: Oh! I wish I could do something about it! But I am happy to have you as my friend.
Toby: Thank you, Monika!
Monika: Er... could you tell me something more about yourself?
Toby: Why not! What do you want to know about me, Monika?
Monika: What do you eat for breakfast?
Toby: Well, we Martians don't need to eat anything. We get our energy from the Sun.
Monika: Oh! Don't you ever feel like having ice creams and chocolates? I can't think of life without them!
Toby: No, Monika! We are made of different stuff.
Monika: Okay, tell me one thing. How are you able to see with only one eye? I see only one big eye in the centre of your face.
Toby: My friend, we Martians need only one eye to see clearly. In fact, I can see even with my eye closed. I can see beyond mountains and clouds, wood and even metals. I can see that you are carrying a toy in your pocket. It's a blue aeroplane. Isn't it? And yes! That apple you just had for breakfast is going round-and-round in your stomach right now!
Monika: (gasps in disbelief) Oh my goodness!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between Monika and Toby. Toby is a Martian and is stranded on Earth. He has become Monika's friend. As you listen, put a tick mark against the correct statements and a cross against the wrong ones.

question_bank.stem: Toby misses his school.
Monika accepts Toby as her friend.
Monika asks Toby about one of his facial features.
Toby tells Monika that she had a banana for breakfast.
Toby shares that he can see much clearer with a closed eye.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to the conversation again and fill in the blanks with the exact words you hear.

question_bank.stem: Toby: I miss my _________________ and home.
Toby: Well, we _________________ don't need to eat anything. We get our _________________ from the Sun.
Monika: Oh! Don't you ever feel like having ice-creams and _________________!
Monika: Okay, tell me one thing. How are you able to see only with _________________ eye?
Toby: I can see beyond mountains and _________________, woods and even ____________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following words from the text aloud and circle the letters that are not pronounced (silent letters): ballet (ba-lay), alarm (uh-laam), whistle (wi-sl), psychologist (sai-ko-luh-juhst). Now, read some more words with silent letters. As you read, circle the letters that are not pronounced: receipt, debris, pneumonia, island, wreck, aisle, almond, campaign, foreign, fasten.

Work in pairs. Use the given situations to ask for and give suggestions.
• You need to make a model of the solar system for the Science Exhibition but don't know what materials to use. Ask your friend for suggestions.
• You need to prepare for the inter-class quiz on the topic Chandrayaan-3. Ask your friend for advice on how to prepare.
You may use the prompts given below for asking for advice (What should I do…? How can I…? etc.) and giving advice (I feel you should…; You should ensure…; etc.).

Complete the social media web given below with the uses of the internet. Now, write an article titled 'The Importance of Internet' with the help of the social media web.
Points to remember:
Heading/Title; Name of the writer; Paragraph 1: Give a general introduction to the topic; Paragraph 2: Explain the uses of the internet and why it is important. Give examples to support your view; Paragraph 3: End with a comment and leave the reader with a thoughtful idea or quote.

Choose the appropriate adjectives from the box given below to match with the words 1–8 that follow: nagging, vague, delighted, narrow, sluggish, hushed, inexplicable, sheepish.
1. ________________ path  2. ________________ worry  3. ________________ plan  4. ________________ look  5. ________________ reason  6. ________________ traffic  7. ________________ voices  8. _______________ expression
Now, underline the sentences that use these adjectives in the text.

question_bank.stem: Match each adjective from the box to the correct word: (1) path, (2) worry, (3) plan, (4) look, (5) reason, (6) traffic, (7) voices, (8) expression.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
The word 'intercom' is used in the text. It is called a merged word as it is made by combining the parts of two words—'internal' and 'communication'. Merged words are made by combining two words. Combine the words in Column 1 with the words in Column 2 to make merged words. Write the merged word and its meaning.
Column 1: motor, smoke, situation, video, spoon, web  |  Column 2: hotel, fog, comedy, log, fork, seminar

question_bank.stem: motor + hotel = ?
smoke + fog = ?
situation + comedy = ?
video + log = ?
spoon + fork = ?
web + seminar = ?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the following sentence from the text and fill in the blank: 'It will mean that you're being doubly safeguarded.' In the given sentence, 'will' denotes __________ (past/present/future) time.
Study the table listing usages of future time references, then complete the following exercises.

question_bank.stem: In the given sentence, 'will' denotes __________ (past/present/future) time.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Fill in the blanks with the correct form (Simple Present, Present Progressive, 'will', or 'going to') to indicate future time.
1. Our flight __________ (leave) at 10.00 a.m. tomorrow.
2. They __________ (finish) the project by the end of this week.
3. The weather forecast says it __________ (rain) later today.
4. We __________ (move) into our new house next month.
5. I __________ (start) my new hobby class next Monday.
6. She __________ (go) to the mall this afternoon.
7. The team __________ (have) a meeting at 3.00 p.m. tomorrow.
8. I __________ (help) you with your homework after dinner.

question_bank.stem: Our flight __________ (leave) at 10.00 a.m. tomorrow.
They __________ (finish) the project by the end of this week.
The weather forecast says it __________ (rain) later today.
We __________ (move) into our new house next month.
I __________ (start) my new hobby class next Monday.
She __________ (go) to the mall this afternoon.
The team __________ (have) a meeting at 3.00 p.m. tomorrow.
I __________ (help) you with your homework after dinner.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks with the suitable forms of verbs given in brackets to express future time reference. (AI paragraph passage with 6 blanks: revolutionise, develop, transform, open, assist, be)

question_bank.stem: By 2040, artificial intelligence 1. ________ (revolutionise) medical treatments.
Researchers 2.________ (develop) AI-powered diagnostic tools that 3. ________ (transform) healthcare by the end of this decade.
Next week, the International Science Congress 4. _______ (open) to showcase advancements in AI and robotics.
In the coming years, robots 5. _______ (assist) surgeons in performing complex operations.
Years ago, experts had predicted that AI would reshape our world, and now, as we approach the future, this 6. _______ (be) our new reality.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: 'A canary in the coal mine' is a popular phrase to describe early signs of potential danger. Read about how coal miners in places like Arunachal Pradesh used canary birds to detect the presence of carbon monoxide. These birds are particularly sensitive to carbon monoxide and can prevent mining accidents.

Astronauts living in space stations have to do things differently. Read how they manage the routine tasks in the absence of gravity (Write, Exercise, Eat, Sleep). You may find out more information about it from the internet.

Did you know that animals can save humans from natural calamities? Animals can sense natural calamities. Read the following examples and decide if this is true: elephants, flamingos, dogs before 2004 tsunami; oxen, sheep, dogs, geese before Naples quake 1805; migratory birds dodging storms; sharks and dolphins sensing hurricanes.
Read about Salim Ali, the 'Birdman of India'—India's first ornithologist to conduct systematic bird surveys. He was awarded the Padma Bhushan (1958) and the Padma Vibhushan (1976).
We need to conserve our 'feathered' friends. Work in groups of four. Look at the pictures and make a bird feeder with cardboard, chart paper, etc.
